title: Results
text: This section compares three control strategies in the custom rocket landing environment: a random policy baseline, Deep Q-Learning (DQN), and Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). All agents were trained using Ray RLlib under identical environment dynamics and reward structure, allowing for direct behavioral and learning-curve comparisons.

title: Results
text:
Random Policy



Random Gif


The random policy selects one of the four discrete actions uniformly at random at every timestep. It provides a baseline for evaluating whether learned behavior is meaningfully better than chance.

Observations

The rocket frequently tumbles due to uncoordinated rotation actions.
Thrust is applied inconsistently, often increasing instability rather than correcting it.
Landings are almost always crashes, typically at high velocity or extreme angles.
Episode rewards remain strongly negative due to cumulative tilt, velocity, and terminal crash penalties.

This confirms that the environment is non-trivial. Stable landings require coordinated control over both translation and rotation, which random exploration alone cannot achieve.

title: Results
text:
Deep Q-Learning Networks (DQN)



DQN Gif





DQN Training Curves


DQN learns an action-value function mapping each state to expected future reward for each discrete action. Through experience replay and target network stabilization, it gradually improves control behavior over training iterations.

Behavioral Results

The agent learns to remain more upright during descent.
Thruster firing becomes more purposeful, typically used to counteract excessive downward velocity.
Horizontal drift is partially corrected, though not always precisely.
Successful soft landings emerge after sufficient training, though with occasional instability.

DQN demonstrates that value-based methods can solve this control problem despite the continuous state space. However, learned policies sometimes appear reactive rather than anticipatory, especially under higher angular momentum.


Training Dynamics
The DQN training curves show:

Mean episode reward steadily increases over time.
Early training is highly volatile due to exploratory behavior.
As epsilon decays and Q-values stabilize, variance decreases.
Convergence occurs once the agent reliably achieves soft landings in a majority of episodes.

The learning curve reflects the typical DQN pattern: unstable early exploration followed by gradual policy refinement as replay memory accumulates diverse experiences.

title: Results
text:
Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)



PPO Gif





PPO Training Curves


PPO directly optimizes the policy using clipped gradient updates, improving stability compared to unconstrained policy gradient methods. Because it is on-policy, it relies on fresh trajectory rollouts collected each iteration.

Behavioral Results

The rocket maintains near-vertical orientation throughout descent.
Engine burns are smoother and more anticipatory than DQN.
Horizontal corrections are applied earlier and more consistently.
Soft landings are more repeatable and visually controlled.

Compared to DQN, PPO produces smoother control trajectories. The descent often resembles a disciplined landing sequence: stabilize orientation, reduce vertical speed, correct lateral offset, and gently settle onto the pad.


Training Dynamics
The PPO training curves show:

Faster reward growth during early training.
Lower variance between iterations compared to DQN.
More stable convergence once landing strategies are discovered.
Consistent high rewards once the agent internalizes the shaped reward structure.

Because PPO updates the policy directly while constraining step size, it tends to avoid the oscillatory value overestimation sometimes observed in Q-learning.

title: Results
text:
Limitations
Despite successful learning, several limitations remain:

The simulation is simplified to 2D and omits aerodynamic drag and stochastic disturbances.
The action space is discrete rather than continuous thrust modulation.
Generalization to unseen initial conditions was not extensively evaluated.
Reward shaping may bias the agent toward specific landing styles rather than optimal fuel efficiency.

Additionally, performance is sensitive to hyperparameters such as learning rate, exploration schedule, and reward scaling.

title: Results
text:
Future Directions
Several extensions could further strengthen the project:

Transition to a continuous action space using algorithms such as SAC or TD3.
Introduce wind disturbances or randomized initial conditions to test robustness.
Add fuel consumption penalties to encourage energy-efficient landings.
Extend the environment to 3D dynamics.
Incorporate curriculum learning to gradually increase task difficulty.

This project provides a foundation for exploring more realistic autonomous control problems and demonstrates how reinforcement learning can approximate sophisticated aerospace maneuvers through trial-and-error optimization alone.

title: Self Landing Rocket Using Reinforcement Learning
text:
Rocket landing is a challenging nonlinear control problem. A vehicle descending under gravity must regulate position, velocity, and orientation simultaneously, while operating under actuator and fuel constraints. Small control errors compound quickly, making a stable touchdown difficult without precise feedback and coordination.
This project explores whether reinforcement learning can learn that control strategy directly from interaction with a simulated environment. Using a custom 2D physics engine built with Gymnasium and rendered in PyGame, agents are trained to land a rocket upright and within a defined pad region. Training is performed with Ray RLlib using both DQN and PPO. The learned policies discover when to rotate, when to fire the main engine, and how to reduce velocity for a controlled landing without any hardcoded landing logic.

title: Self Landing Rocket Using Reinforcement Learning
text:
Rocket landing is a challenging nonlinear control problem. A vehicle descending under gravity must regulate position, velocity, and orientation simultaneously, while operating under actuator and fuel constraints. Small control errors compound quickly, making a stable touchdown difficult without precise feedback and coordination.
This project explores whether reinforcement learning can learn that control strategy directly from interaction with a simulated environment. Using a custom 2D physics engine built with Gymnasium and rendered in PyGame, agents are trained to land a rocket upright and within a defined pad region. Training is performed with Ray RLlib using both DQN and PPO. The learned policies discover when to rotate, when to fire the main engine, and how to reduce velocity for a controlled landing without any hardcoded landing logic.

title: Self Landing Rocket Using Reinforcement Learning
text:
Motivation
Reinforcement learning treats landing as a sequential decision problem. The agent observes the rocket’s full state, takes discrete thrust actions, and receives shaped rewards encouraging stability, fuel-aware control, and precise touchdown. Over many episodes, it learns a control policy purely from reward feedback.
The project is built around four core design elements:

A 7-dimensional continuous state space capturing position, velocity, angle, angular velocity, and remaining fuel

A shaped reward function with dense mid-air penalties and tiered landing bonuses

Distributed RLlib training pipelines for both DQN and PPO

A discrete thruster-based action space reflecting realistic actuation choices

By combining structured reward shaping with modern deep RL algorithms, the agent learns coordinated behaviors such as stabilizing orientation early, correcting horizontal drift during descent, and reducing velocity near the ground before committing to landing.

title: Self Landing Rocket Using Reinforcement Learning
text:
GitHub Repository
The GitHub repository hosting all code for this project can be found here.

title: Methods
text:
The environment is a custom Gymnasium implementation simulating 2D rigid-body rocket dynamics with fuel constraints and rotational control. At each timestep, the agent observes a 7-dimensional continuous state vector and selects one of four discrete thrust actions. An episode terminates if the rocket lands, crashes, or exits the horizontal boundary.
The environment follows the Gymnasium API and supports both human rendering via PyGame and RGB array output for video generation.



The simulation uses simplified 2D rigid-body dynamics with the following constants:

Gravity: -0.05
Main engine thrust: 0.20
Angular thrust: 0.05
Angular damping: 0.99 multiplier per step
Timestep: dt = 0.05
Horizontal boundary: |x| ≤ 1.5
Vertical ceiling: y ≤ 1.95



At each step:

Gravity continuously accelerates the rocket downward.
The main engine applies thrust along the rocket’s longitudinal axis.
Rotation actions apply angular thrust, modifying angular velocity.
Angular velocity is slightly damped each step for stability.
Position and angle are integrated forward using simple Euler updates.
Fuel decreases by 0.01 per main engine burn and is bounded to [0, 1].

The rocket’s angle is wrapped to [-π, π] to maintain numerical stability.





The observation vector consists of seven variables:



Variable
Description
Range




x
Horizontal position
[-1.5, 1.5]


y
Vertical position
[-1.95, 1.95]


vx
Horizontal velocity
[-2.0, 2.0]


vy
Vertical velocity
[-2.0, 2.0]


θ
Angle from vertical
[-π, π]


ω
Angular velocity
[-2.0, 2.0]


fuel
Remaining fuel fraction
[0.0, 1.0]



Initial conditions are randomized at reset:

x ∈ [-0.5, 0.5]
y ∈ [0.8, 1.2]
θ ∈ [-0.3, 0.3]
Velocities initialized to zero
fuel = 1.0

This randomization ensures the agent must generalize across multiple initial configurations.




The agent selects one of four discrete actions:



Action ID
Description
Effect




0
No-op
Freefall


1
Rotate left
Increase angular velocity


2
Main engine
Apply thrust along rocket axis and consume fuel


3
Rotate right
Decrease angular velocity



Rotation affects angular velocity directly. The main engine modifies both horizontal and vertical velocity components based on the current rocket angle.

The agent selects one of four discrete actions:



Action ID
Description
Effect




0
No-op
Freefall


1
Rotate left
Increase angular velocity

Main engine
Apply thrust along rocket axis and consume fuel

Rotate right
Decrease angular velocity



Rotation affects angular velocity directly. The main engine modifies both horizontal and vertical velocity components based on the current rocket angle.

title: Methods
text:
Per-Step Reward Components
At every timestep:

Penalize tilt using squared angle:

-0.15 * angle^2

Penalize angular velocity:

-0.05 * |ω|

Penalize horizontal drift:

-0.3 * |x|

Penalize overall speed using squared velocity:

-0.2 * (vx^2 + vy^2)

Encourage descent:

+0.1 * (1.0 - y)

Apply small survival penalty:

-0.05

title: Methods
text:
Near-Ground Shaping (y < 0.3)
Additional shaping encourages precise landings:

Reward slow vertical speed
Reward low horizontal velocity
Reward upright orientation

This creates a structured learning signal that transitions from general stabilization mid-air to precision control near the landing pad.

title: Methods
text:
Terminal Conditions
An episode terminates if:

The rocket exits horizontal bounds (|x| > 1.5)

Reward: -50

The rocket contacts the ground (y ≤ 0)

Landing outcomes:

Soft landing

|x| < 0.1
|vx| < 0.1
|vy| < 0.2
|angle| < 0.1
Reward: +200

Near landing

Relaxed thresholds
Reward: +75

Crash

Reward: -100


This tiered reward structure improves learning stability compared to purely binary success signals.

title: Methods
text:
Deep Q-Network (DQN)
Type: Off-policy, value-based

Architecture and Features

Double Q-learning
Dueling network architecture
Experience replay buffer (capacity 50,000)
Target network updates every 500 steps



Key Hyperparameters

gamma = 0.99
lr = 1e-3
train_batch_size = 3000
6 environment runners × 4 environments each



Stopping Criteria
Training stops at:

150 iterations, or
Mean episode return ≥ 150

The best-performing checkpoint (based on episode return) is automatically saved and exported for evaluation and visualization.

title: Methods
text:
Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)
Type: On-policy, policy-gradient

Architecture and Features

Clipped objective (clip_param = 0.2)
Generalized Advantage Estimation
Entropy regularization (entropy_coeff = 0.01)
Value loss coefficient (vf_loss_coeff = 0.5)



Key Hyperparameters

gamma = 0.99
lr = 1e-4
train_batch_size = 6000
minibatch_size = 128
num_epochs = 10
8 environment runners × 4 environments each



Stopping Criteria
Training stops at:

200 iterations, or
Mean episode return ≥ 175

As with DQN, the best checkpoint is automatically selected and exported.
